content.text: Outcome metrics linked to value streams are the mechanism that connects AI investment to business results. Without this linkage, AI programmes produce activity metrics (models deployed, tokens generated, throughput increased) that look impressive in CoE reports but leave the CFO and ESC unable to answer the question that matters: how much of the improvement in the income statement or balance sheet is attributable to AI? The metrics hierarchy ensures that every AI intervention measure connects upward to a business KPI.

attrs.alt: Outcome metrics hierarchy from platform to business outcome

content.text: Every value stream produces metrics at four levels. The levels are causally linked: platform metrics enable AI interventions; AI intervention metrics drive value stream improvements; value stream KPIs move business outcomes. Defining metrics at all four levels and making the causal links explicit prevents the common failure of optimising at the wrong level.
The highest level — the metrics the CFO, board, and ESC actually care about. These are the measures in the investment case that justified the AI programme. Examples:
— Sales cycle length, win rate, upsell rate, revenue per customer.
— Cost per transaction, cost-to-serve per customer, headcount required per unit of output.
— Net Promoter Score, Customer Effort Score, first-contact resolution rate.
— Fraud loss rate, compliance incident rate, model risk incidents per quarter.

Business outcome KPIs are almost never 100% attributable to AI — pricing, market conditions, and process changes contribute independently. The attribution analysis (Level 3 intervention metrics compared to a counterfactual) is what isolates the AI contribution.
These measure the end-to-end performance of the value stream that AI is transforming. They connect directly to the business outcome but are more operationally specific:
Days to first active use; abandonment rate; support tickets in first 30 days
Days FNOL to settlement; cycle time per step; rework rate per step
Lead time from commit to production; change failure rate; MTTR
Days to qualified; proposal-to-close rate; average deal velocity
Forecast accuracy (%); stockout rate; excess inventory days
Days to close; restatement rate; manual journal entry volume
These measure the AI system's direct performance within the value stream step it was applied to. They answer: 'Is the AI doing what it was designed to do?'
% of eligible cases handled by AI without human escalation
% of AI outputs that are correct per the ground-truth label
% of AI recommendations rejected by the human reviewer
Gap between AI confidence score and actual accuracy
Disparity in accuracy across demographic subgroups
The foundation layer — infrastructure health that enables the levels above. Failures here cascade upward:

— The AI gateway (which routes, rate-limits, and audits model calls) must meet the SLA of every value stream it serves. A gateway at 99.0% uptime takes down all AI-assisted steps for ~88 hours per year.
— Economics of the AI portfolio. Optimised prompts and caching reduce cost; unchecked growth signals prompt engineering problems.
— Statistical distance between the model's training distribution and the current inference distribution. Rising drift predicts accuracy degradation.
— % of model outputs that pass the safety and policy guardrails before reaching the end user. Declining pass rate signals prompt injection attempts or distributional shift.
For each metric at every level, the operational specification must define six elements before the metric is tracked:
% of FNOL submissions with ≥1 data field requiring correction within 48h of submission
Where the raw data comes from
Claims management system error log, extracted daily
Who is accountable for the metric value
Pre-AI value used to calculate improvement
18.3% (average of the 6 months before AI deployment)
Value at which the AI initiative is considered successful
Metrics without an operational specification are decoration. If two people reading the definition would calculate different values from the same raw data, the specification is incomplete.

Business outcomes improve for many reasons simultaneously — process changes, market conditions, pricing, staff turnover. Isolating the AI contribution requires a principled attribution method. Three approaches are used in practice:
Split eligible cases: AI-assisted vs. control (standard process). Compare outcomes.
High volume, near-identical cases; randomisation is feasible
Compare post-deployment metrics to pre-deployment baseline, adjusted for expected counterfactual trend
High volume; A/B not feasible; reasonable counterfactual estimate available
Compare the AI-treated BU to an untreated BU with similar baseline trends
Multiple BUs; one piloting AI while others are control groups

import statistics

def estimate_ai_attribution(
    pre_ai_values: list[float],
    post_ai_values: list[float],
    counterfactual_trend_pct: float = 0.0,
) -> dict:
    """
    Estimate what fraction of observed improvement is attributable to AI
    vs. counterfactual improvement (seasonal trend, other initiatives).

    pre_ai_values: metric values for N periods before AI deployment
    post_ai_values: metric values for N periods after AI deployment
    counterfactual_trend_pct: expected improvement % per period without AI
    """
    pre_mean = statistics.mean(pre_ai_values)
    post_mean = statistics.mean(post_ai_values)
    total_change = pre_mean - post_mean  # lower is better (cycle time)

    periods = len(post_ai_values)
    counterfactual_change = pre_mean * (counterfactual_trend_pct / 100) * periods

    ai_attributable = total_change - counterfactual_change
    ai_fraction = (ai_attributable / total_change * 100) if total_change != 0 else 0

    return {
        "pre_mean": round(pre_mean, 2),
        "post_mean": round(post_mean, 2),
        "total_change": round(total_change, 2),
        "counterfactual_change": round(counterfactual_change, 2),
        "ai_attributable_change": round(ai_attributable, 2),
        "ai_attribution_pct": round(ai_fraction, 1),
    }

# FNOL cycle time attribution
result = estimate_ai_attribution(
    pre_ai_values=[12.1, 11.9, 12.3, 12.0, 11.8],  # days, 5 pre-AI periods
    post_ai_values=[9.2, 8.8, 8.5, 8.1, 7.9],       # days, 5 post-AI periods
    counterfactual_trend_pct=0.5,  # 0.5% per-period improvement expected without AI
)
print("Attribution analysis:")
for k, v in result.items():
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")
# Attribution analysis:
#   pre_mean: 12.02
#   post_mean: 8.5
#   total_change: 3.52
#   counterfactual_change: 0.3
#   ai_attributable_change: 3.22
#   ai_attribution_pct: 91.5

The counterfactual trend adjustment is critical. A claims cycle time that was already improving 1% per quarter due to process reengineering should not have that improvement credited to AI. Estimate the counterfactual using pre-AI trend data, industry benchmarks, or expert judgment — then subtract it from the total improvement before claiming AI attribution.
Any metric outside SLA triggers P2 incident
Accuracy < threshold or override rate > 15% triggers model review
KPI miss > 10% vs. target triggers escalation to Sponsor
Sustained miss vs. investment case triggers kill/continue decision
Measuring AI activity, not business outcomes
— Reporting 'tokens generated per day' or 'models deployed' to the ESC is activity measurement. The ESC cannot connect these to business value. Replace with Level 1 or Level 2 metrics.
— Deploying AI without measuring the pre-AI state makes improvement claims unverifiable. Instrument the value stream steps before deploying AI, not after.

— Reporting 95% accuracy without specifying the class distribution or the cost of false positives versus false negatives is misleading. A model that classifies 95% of cases correctly but misclassifies every fraud case has zero business value.

— Human override rate is the most honest AI effectiveness metric for Augment use cases. If humans reject 40% of AI recommendations, either the AI is wrong or the humans don't trust it — both are problems.

— Treating a platform metric (gateway uptime) as an outcome metric creates confusion. When gateway uptime is Green but value stream cycle time is Red, the diagnosis is obscured if both are presented in the same metric tier.

When a GenAI programme spans multiple value streams and BUs, maintaining metric specifications in scattered documents produces inconsistency. A metric registry — a structured data store of all metric specifications — solves this:
One row per metric, with all six operational specification fields populated before the metric goes live.
A flag for whether the metric has a confirmed baseline (no baseline = red flag; metric is not yet trackable).
A linkage field connecting each Level 3 AI metric to its parent Level 2 value stream KPI and its grandparent Level 1 business outcome.
A review date field — metrics without a review in the past 6 months are stale and must be re-validated.

The PMO owns the metric registry. The CoE Lead is responsible for ensuring Level 3 and 4 metrics are populated before any use case exits the Pilot phase. BU owners are responsible for Level 1 and 2 metrics being confirmed and baselined before the use case enters Scale.
Worked Example: End-to-End Metrics for Insurance FNOL

This section walks through the complete metric set for a single value stream — FNOL (First Notice of Loss) to settlement in property insurance — at all four levels. The AI interventions are: automated triage classification (Automate), recommended settlement amounts (Augment), and fraud propensity scoring (Insight).
Faster settlement via auto-triage and auto-settlement
Auto-triage removes 3 days from intake bottleneck
AI validation flags missing fields at submission
Shared platform for all three models

Notice the causal chain: gateway uptime (L4) enables inference latency (L4), which enables high triage handling rate (L3), which reduces intake bottleneck (L2 cycle time), which reduces cost per case and improves NPS (L1). A problem at any level breaks the chain downstream — making this explicit in a metrics hierarchy is what allows rapid root-cause identification.
Attribution Analysis for This Value Stream
After 6 months, the L2 cycle time has improved from 12 to 8.5 days. Before crediting AI, the team applies counterfactual adjustment:
Process re-engineering (parallel investigation vs. sequential) was also deployed in month 2 — estimated to contribute 1.0 day reduction independently.
Seasonal claim volume dip in months 3–4 contributed approximately 0.3 days reduction historically.
AI-attributable improvement: 12 − 8.5 − 1.0 − 0.3 = 2.2 days (63% of total improvement).
This 2.2-day AI contribution is presented to the ESC as the AI programme's L2 value, with methodology documented.

Without counterfactual adjustment, the team would have claimed full credit for 3.5 days of improvement — overstating AI impact by 37%. Over-claiming erodes credibility when future programmes are benchmarked against inflated prior results.

A recurring failure pattern is instrumenting the value stream after AI is deployed — at which point no pre-AI baseline exists, attribution is impossible, and the ESC cannot evaluate the investment. The correct sequence is:
— Install telemetry on the value stream steps targeted for AI. Run for at least one full business cycle (3–6 months) to establish a stable baseline.
— Lock the baseline values in the metric registry before the AI system is deployed. Post-deployment adjustments to baselines require PMO and Sponsor sign-off.
— Document expected improvement rates without AI (trend, known process changes, seasonal patterns). This is the denominator for attribution.
— The AI system goes live. The metric collection continues uninterrupted using the same telemetry.
— Compare post-deployment values to baseline, subtract counterfactual, and report net AI attribution to the ESC.
— Model drift, accuracy decay, and cost-per-unit trends may cause value to erode over time. Sustained tracking catches this before it reaches the L1 business outcomes.

This sequence requires the CoE Lead and the value stream owner to jointly design the metric framework before the AI use case is approved for development — not after go-live. The metric design review is a formal gate in the use case intake process.

The four-level metrics hierarchy links platform health (L4) through AI intervention performance (L3) to value stream KPIs (L2) and ultimately to business outcomes (L1). Every metric must have a defined causal path to a business outcome.
Each metric requires a six-part operational specification: name, definition, data source, owner, baseline, and target. Without a specification, the metric is unmeasurable.
Attribution of AI contribution requires separating AI impact from counterfactual improvement. Use controlled A/B tests where feasible; pre/post with counterfactual adjustment otherwise.
Human override rate is the single most honest effectiveness metric for Augment use cases — high override rates indicate model quality or trust problems that dashboard accuracy often hides.
The metric registry — owned by PMO — stores all metric specifications, baseline confirmations, and level linkages. A metric without a confirmed baseline is not ready for production tracking.
